item.title: How to break the ransomware-as-a-service model: what the official sources actually say
item.description: Ransomware today is a service industry, not a lone wolf: operators who rent the malware, affiliates who strike, brokers who sell the initial access. This explainer is not the attack of the day: it lines up what official sources say — CISA with #StopRansomware, Europol with the No More Ransom project, ENISA with its Threat Landscape — on how that chain breaks. The takeaway is less spectacular than a ransom and more useful: you hit the model's economics, not the single group.

item.title: No malware, no ransom, no patch: the KNXlock case
item.description: On 15 July 2026 CISA added to the KEV catalog a 2023 CVE describing a 2021 attack. There is no malicious code: the attackers purge a building's KNX devices, then set the BCU key — the password the protocol provides as a protective feature. Vendors replied that no reset exists. No ransom ever arrived.

item.title: FortiBleed: there is no patch, because there is no vulnerability
item.description: Roughly half of all internet-facing Fortinet firewalls have their admin credentials in the hands of an access broker. There is no CVE to patch: there are exported config files, SHA-256 hashes cracked offline, and perfectly legitimate logins. In July, SOCRadar tied the operation to the INC and Lynx ransomware brands.

item.title: Interlock: the ransomware that walks in the front door
item.description: Interlock flipped the ransomware script: no phishing, no breached VPN. The victim visits a legitimate compromised site, sees a fake CAPTCHA, and pastes the command that infects them. Anatomy of an attack chain in which the user is the exploit.

item.title: The QR code that steals your session, not your password
item.description: Kimsuky stopped fighting corporate email filters. It simply changed channel: a QR code forces the victim onto a personal phone, outside the IT perimeter. And it steals the session token rather than the password — so MFA falls without raising a single alert.

item.title: Miasma: valid SLSA provenance for malicious packages
item.description: Thirty-two Red Hat npm packages were published with malicious payloads — carrying valid SLSA provenance attestations. The technology built to guarantee build integrity certified the malware, because SLSA attests where a package was built, not whether the build's input was legitimate.

item.title: "Exploitation Less Likely", then three days to patch
item.description: Microsoft rated it "Exploitation Less Likely". Five weeks later CISA put it in the KEV catalog with a three-day remediation window. A lesson in what vendor exploitability predictions are actually worth.
